segments.s253-g9: This noun phrase names the actors: بزرگان (plural) 'the great ones/nobles' + توران 'of Turan' (a genitive association). گشاده کمر is a postpositive adjectival phrase literally 'open/loosened waist' — an idiomatic description 'with loosened belts/girdles' where گشاده modifies کمر and the whole phrase qualifies بزرگان.
segments.s253-g10: This prepositional phrase says where and to whom: به پیش = 'toward/in the presence of' (به + پیش). سپهدار is the addressee/title 'the commander'. بر خاک سر is a poetic word order for سر بر خاک 'head(s) upon the dust/ground' (بر = 'upon', خاک = 'dust/ground', سر = 'head'); the verb is omitted and understood (they cast/fell their heads on the ground).
segments.s253-g11: همه here functions adverbially as 'entirely/altogether' modifying the verbal phrase. جنگ را is the definite object 'the war' (را marks it). پاک بسته is a compound where پاک (completely/cleanly) qualifies بسته 'sealed/closed/ended' — together 'completely brought the war to a close'. میان 'between' indicates the relation (ellipsis: between the two sides).
segments.s253-g12: This noun phrase states the emotional state of the Iranians: همه دل = 'every/ all the hearts' (همه = 'all/entire'), پر از = 'full of' (پر followed by از), کین = 'hatred/vengeance' and ایرانیان is the possessor 'of the Iranians'. Word order is typical Persian: determiner/adjective + noun + complement + possessor.
segments.s253-g13: This is a relative/causal clause plus the noun it qualifies: کز = 'that/which/since', اندازه بگذشت = 'passed measure/ exceeded bounds' (بگذشت is past 'passed/exceeded'), ما را سخن = 'speech for us' or 'what we may say' (ما را marks the beneficiary/possessor). In Persian poetry the relative clause can precede the noun it qualifies.
segments.s253-g14: This interrogative clause asks what should be done: چه = 'what', افگند is the root 'to throw/cast' (here an infinitival/nominal sense 'to cast'), باید is the modal 'must/ought to'. بدین is 'to this' (ب- prefix + دین as an archaic/demonstrative element; together 'to/into this'), کار = 'affair/deed', and بن is not used literally here as 'root' but functions idiomatically/poetically (see note). The literal sense-listing supplied does not fit دین and بن exactly; syntactically بدین کار = 'into this affair' and the clause asks 'what must be cast into this affair?'.
segments.s253-g15: کزین = 'from this' (a fused preposition + demonstrative), ننگ = 'shame/disgrace'. بر introduces the locus 'upon/on' (بر شاه و گردنکشان = 'upon the king and the rebels/proud ones'). The conjunctive و links شاه and گردنکشان; in context گردنکشان denotes the rebellious/proud faction.
segments.s253-g16: بماند is the present/subjunctive/optative form 'remains/will remain' (third person). ز = 'from' (poetic variant of از), کردار = 'deed/action', بیژن the proper name 'Bijan', and نشان = 'sign/token/trace' — the clause means 'a sign/trace remains from Bijan's deed.' Word order (verb before the prepositional phrase) reflects poetic emphasis.
segments.s253-g33: بدو is the old dative/adverbial 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (third singular), with an implicit subject (he). ما زین نداریم is a negated present ('we do not have this saddle/this [thing]'): ما = 'we', زین = 'this saddle/this thing', نداریم = 'we do not have' (present). باک is a noun 'fear' placed after the clause and functions as the thing denied (we have no fear). Together the clause is direct speech introduced by 'he said to him'.
segments.s253-g34: همی here is an emphatic/old poetic particle preceding the verb, marking resolution/continuity ('indeed/will'); جنگ را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the battle'); برفشانیم is a first-person-plural present/optative-form meaning 'we scatter/drive to pieces' (stem + present ending); خاک is the object 'dust' (so 'scatter the battle to dust'). The verb agrees with the previously stated subject ما ('we').
segments.s253-g35: بنه is a noun 'bundle / small heap' and is the object of the action; با + منیژه marks accompaniment ('with Manijeh'); گسی is an indefinite modifier 'some / a certain' and here helps mean 'a certain (bundle)'; کرد is the past-tense verb 'did / made' (third singular). و بار links to the next clause: بار 'load' (the thing to be borne or buckled on). The sequence therefore describes preparing a bundle/load with Manijeh (the agent is the same implicit subject).
segments.s253-g36: بپوشید (پ+ پوشید) is a past-tense form 'he put on / he donned' (the prefix پ/ب here is the older verbal prefix found in epic style); خود is a possessive/emphatic 'his own'; جامه is the noun 'garment'; کارزار is a noun modifying جامه ('battle' or 'of battle'), so جامهٔ کارزار = 'battle-garment' or 'garb for the fight'. Together: 'he donned his battle-robe.'
segments.s253-g37: ببالا is an adverb 'upward / on high' placed before the verb; برآمد is the past 'rose / went up'; سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the host/army'); بدید is the past 'he saw'. Syntactically the line has two actions of the same subject: 'he rose up' and then 'he saw the host' (سپه را is the object of بدید, not of برآمد).
segments.s253-g38: خروشی is a noun 'a roar' (the sound uttered); چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; شیر is 'lion' and ژیان is an attributive noun (epithet) attached to شیر (here functioning as an intensifier 'living/mighty'), forming the image 'like a lion (full of life/ferocity)'; برکشید is the past 'he raised/uttered' (literally 'lifted up' but used of raising a cry). The phrase thus means 'he uttered a roar like a (mighty) lion.'
segments.s253-g39: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain (person)'; داستان here is used with زد in the idiom داستان زدن — 'to utter / to sing / to chant a tale or cry' (so داستان is the thing uttered, not 'story' in a modern prose sense); زد is the past 'struck/uttered' (third singular); سوار دلیر is a noun phrase 'a brave rider' that identifies the one who did the action. Word order is epic/poetic: 'one uttered a tale — a brave rider (did so).'
segments.s253-g40: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (here giving the content of the cry). روبه is the noun 'fox' as subject of this small clause; چه is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what / how' used to introduce the comparison; سنجد is the noun 'oleaster' (a small, fragile thing used metaphorically here); بچنگال is a prepositional compound 'ب + چنگال' = 'in the grip / in the claw of'; شیر is 'lion'. The clause is elliptical (no copula) and reads roughly 'what is the fox — [but] an oleaster in the lion's claw?' — a proverbial/comparative image.
segments.s253-g105: چنان is an intensifier 'so/thus' placed before the predicate. تیره‌گون شد is the past tense of شدن ('became') with تیره‌گون as a predicative adjective 'dark‑hued'; the verb شد is perfect/past (3rd persons) and completes the copular idea. ز + گرد آفتاب is an ablative/causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of the round radiance of the sun' (ز marks cause in poetic diction).
segments.s253-g106: که introduces a result/comparison clause here ('so that/that one would say'). گفتی is a classical, parenthetical/impersonal use meaning roughly 'one would say' or 'it seemed' (an archaic/modal usage rather than the given noun sense). همی is the old continuative/emphatic particle placed before the verb to indicate continued state. غرقه ماند is the compound action 'remained drenched' (غرقه used adjectivally and ماند 'remained'); در آب is the locative prepositional phrase 'in water'.
segments.s253-g107: ببستند is past plural 'they tied / they fastened'. بر is the preposition 'on/onto'. پیل is the head noun 'elephant', followed by the adjectival/nominal modifiers رویینه خم ('armoured, curved'); in classical poetic order modifiers follow the noun, so 'رویینه خم' describes the thing placed on the elephant (an armoured curved hump/saddle/cover).
segments.s253-g108: دمیدند is past plural 'they blew' (3rd person). شیپور is the direct object 'the trumpet' (object follows verb, common in poetic word order). با + گاودم is an instrumental phrase 'with a ox/cow‑tail' indicating the means used to sound or strike the trumpet.
segments.s253-g109: ز جوشن is an ablative phrase 'from the cuirasses/armour'. یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'all at once / in one instant'. آهنین here is an adjective used substantively ('iron' = iron weapons/arms); together this yields 'they suddenly drew out iron/weapons from their armour.'
segments.s253-g110: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/pulled' (here 'drew their weapons' or 'drew up'). گردان can mean 'a troop/rank' and functions as the object or result of the action. بروی زمین is a locative phrase 'upon the ground' (note: بروی as written is the prepositional combination 'برِ روی' meaning 'on'; the lemma senses supplied do not list that sense exactly so it is treated as the locative 'on').
segments.s253-g111: بجوشید is an intransitive past 'seethed/boiled' with the verb preceding the subject (inverted V–S order typical in poetry): دشت is the subject 'the plain'. و connects the clauses; بتوفید is another past verb 'it spat/shot forth' and کوه following it is the subject 'the mountain' (again inverted order).
segments.s253-g112: ز بانگ is an ablative 'from the cry/sound'. سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders'. هر دو گروه is a classical expression 'both groups/hosts' (هر here supports the numeral دو to yield 'both'); the whole phrase gives the cause: 'because of the cry of the horsemen of both hosts.'
segments.s253-g153: بلشکرگه here is a contracted classical form (بـ + لشکرگه) meaning 'to the army-camp' (directional use of بَـ). آمد is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'he came'. ازان رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from that battlefield' with ازان = 'from that' placed before the location noun رزمگاه.
segments.s253-g154: که introduces a purpose/intent clause here ('so that, in order that'). بخشش is used as 'a granting/donation' (not merely abstract forgiveness). کند is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'may do' (classical narrative uses this form to express intended action). خواسته functions as a past-participial adjective 'requested' (what had been asked), and بر سپاه marks the recipient/beneficiary 'to the army'.
segments.s253-g155: ببخشید is the past 3rd-person singular of بخشیدن and here means 'he bestowed/gave' (not merely 'forgave'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بنهاد is the past 3rd-person singular of نهادن, 'he placed/put' (classical form benhād). بر پیل بار is a prepositional phrase 'on the elephant's load' with بر = 'on/upon', پیل = 'elephant', and بار = 'load/cargo'.
segments.s253-g156: بپیروزی is a prefixed phrase meaning 'with/to victory' (بـ indicating accompaniment or result). آمد is the past 3rd-person singular 'he came'. بر شهریار here means 'to the king' (بر used in the sense 'toward/to'), so the whole phrase indicates arrival before the king while victorious.
segments.s253-g25: Subject first (یلان, 'the heroes/warriors'), then the verb صف کشیدند ('drew up in ranks'); صف کشیدن is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'to form a battle-line.' The phrase بر در سرای is a locative prepositional phrase placed after the verb meaning 'before/at the door/gate of the house/palace' (سرای = an older/formal word for house/palace).
segments.s253-g26: خروش آمد is a simple past/ narrative expression 'a cry/uproar arose' (subject then آمد 'came'); از introduces the source 'from' and links to بوق و هندی, 'trumpet and Indian …'. The final word درای is unclear in the provided senses (likely part of an epithet or instrument-name) and is therefore left uncertain here.
segments.s253-g27: سپاهی ز توران puts the agent/source first: 'an army from Turan.' The verb راند is past 'drove/pushed (on).' بدَان here is best read as the older fused prepositional sense 'به + آن' = 'to that' (to that border), so the phrase means 'drove to that border.' The internal split in the token list reflects the older morphology (ب + دان), with دان not behaving like the verbal 'دان' in the senses given.
segments.s253-g28: که introduces the result/explanatory clause 'such that/so that.' روی زمین = 'the surface of the earth/land' (چسبیده noun + modifier). جز introduces the exception 'except,' and بدریا (here 'to the sea') indicates that everything was driven into the sea; نماند is past negative 'remained not / was left.' Word order places the thing (روی زمین) before the verb نماند for emphasis.
segments.s253-g29: چو here means 'when.' The phrase از دیدگه دیدبان literally 'from the lookout the watchman' places the vantage point (دیدگه) and agent (دیدبان) before the verb; بنگرید (an older narrative form built with the prefix ب-) functions as the past/immediate-past 'looked' or 'cast his gaze.'
segments.s253-g30: زمین را uses the object/definite marker را to topicalize 'the land' before the simile چو دریای جوشان ('like a boiling sea'). The verb بدید is the simple past 'he saw.' Note the two different usages of چو in the sentence: the first is temporal ('when'), the second is comparative ('like').
segments.s253-g31: بر رستم آمد is an idiomatic phrasing 'it came upon Rostam' (i.e., befell him). The conjunction که begins an explanatory clause describing what befell him; ببسیچ modifies کار and denotes a troublesome/complex or obscure affair (an archaic/poetic adjective). کار is the noun 'affair/thing' that wasfalling upon him.
segments.s253-g32: که again introduces the explanatory/result clause. گیتی سیه شد is 'the world became dark' (گیتی subject, سیه شد predicate). ز گرد سوار supplies the cause: ز = 'because of/from' and گرد here means 'dust' (raised by movement), سوار = 'horsemen/riders.' Together: 'the world was darkened by the riders' dust.'
segments.s253-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time-clause. خورشید (subject) + سر برزد is a poetic clause meaning 'the sun raised its head' — برزد is an archaic perfective-type verb here ('appeared/rose'). از کوهسار is a post-verbal prepositional phrase 'from/out of the mountain' specifying origin; word order and the archaic verb give a lofty poetic feel.
segments.s253-g2: Subject سواران توران ('the riders of Turan') precedes the verb; ببستند is a past plural verb meaning 'they bound/tied/packed' (perfective past), and بار is the object 'baggage/load'. The order is straightforward S–V–O in a concise, actional line.
segments.s253-g3: بتوفید is an archaic/poetic past verb ('spat out' / 'vomited forth') preceding its subject (شهر) — a verb–subject inversion common in verse for emphasis. و links the next clause; برآمد خروش is a simple clause 'a roar arose' with برآمد (arose/issued forth) and خروش (roar).
segments.s253-g4: تو گفتی functions as reported speech ('you said'); همی is an archaic particle adding immediacy or emphasis. The following clause is verbless-order-inverted for poetic effect: کر کند نعره گوش = 'the shout makes the ear deaf' where کند is the causative/making verb 'to make', کر 'deaf', نعره 'shout' and گوش 'ear'. Note that classical word order is flexible and verbs or predicates often appear before their objects in poetry.
segments.s253-g5: بدرگاه افراسیاب is a fused prepositional expression 'into the presence/at the court of Afrasiab' (بدرگاه = 'in the presence/at court'), followed by آمدند, a simple past plural 'they came'. The subject is not explicitly repeated; آمدند carries the plural action.
segments.s253-g6: کمربستگان ('the girded ones', i.e., armed warriors) is the subject; بر درش = 'at/on his door/gate' (بر = on/at, در + ش = 'his gate'); صف زدند is an idiomatic compound where زدند (past plural of زدن) is a light verb meaning 'made/formed' so صف زدند = 'they formed a line' (poetic collocation). The verb is past plural agreeing with the subject.
segments.s253-g7: همه ('all/everyone') is the subject; یکسره modifies the following phrase as 'completely/utterly'. جنگ را ساخته uses ساخته (past participle / perfect 'made/prepared') with را marking جنگ as the definite object: 'had made/put together the war' → 'had prepared for war'. The clause conveys completed past action applying to all.
segments.s253-g8: دل is the experiential subject 'the heart(s)'; از بوم و آرام is a compound prepositional phrase 'from homeland and from peace/rest'; پرداخته is a poetic past-participle/verb form meaning 'has departed/left' or 'has been taken away' — together: 'their hearts have departed from home and tranquility.' The sequence uses compact, elevated diction typical of epic poetry.
segments.s253-g137: سران سواران is a genitive/attributive sequence meaning 'the chiefs/leaders of the riders' (noun + noun). چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and is followed by برگ درخت 'the leaf of a tree' (noun + possessive/genitive), so the whole segment = 'the leaders of the horsemen, like the leaf of a tree.'
segments.s253-g138: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase: فرو (down-) + ریخت (fell/poured) = 'fell down' (past action). از introduces cause/origin 'from' and بار here means 'burden/weight'; و links clauses 'and'; برگشت is used as 'reversal/turning' and bخت is 'fortune' — together 'and fortune reversed.' The verb-like و + برگشت + بخت is a poetic inversion (noun after verbal idea).
segments.s253-g139: همه رزمگه ('the whole battlefield') is followed by سربسر, an adverb meaning 'throughout/everywhere', then جوی خون 'a stream of blood' (noun + noun). The phrase uses reversed/plain word order for poetic force: 'throughout the whole battlefield, a stream of blood.'
segments.s253-g140: درفش سپهدار توران is a genitive chain 'the banner (darfash) of the commander of Turan' (noun + title + place), followed by نگون, an adjective/predicative meaning 'overthrown/overturned' or 'ill‑fated' describing the banner. Word order places the descriptive predicate after the noun for emphasis.
segments.s253-g141: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'. چون here introduces a subordinate temporal/causal clause ('when/since'), and بخت‌برگشته is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'fortune-turned' (i.e., that fortune had changed). دید is the past tense 'saw' — so 'when the commander saw that fortune had turned.'
segments.s253-g142: دلیران توران همه کشته دید places the object before the verb in typical Persian order: 'he saw all (همه) the brave men (دلیران) of Turan killed/slain (کشته)'. دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw.' کشته here functions as a predicate/participial adjective meaning 'slain' or as a noun 'the slain.'
segments.s253-g143: بیفگند is a past-tense verb 'he threw/cast' (archaic/poetic form). شمشیر هندی is a noun followed by an adjective/attributive 'Indian' (Indian-made sword). ز + دست means 'from the hand' (the instrument/possessor), so the clause means 'he cast his Indian sword from his hand' (threw it away/dropped it).
segments.s253-g144: یکی is an indefinite 'one/someone' (subject). اسب آسوده‌تر is a noun + comparative adjective 'a calmer/more at-ease horse' (adjective follows the noun). برنشست is past 'he mounted/sat upon' (بر-نشست = 'mounted'). Together: 'one man mounted a calmer steed.'
segments.s253-g49: Azān kūh (ازان کوه) is a prepositional phrase 'from that mountain' with ازان 'from that' before the noun (classical/poetic order). سر 'summit, head' is the noun being described, followed by سوی هامون 'toward the plain' (سوی + goal). کشید is a past verb meaning here 'moved/leaned/stretched' (poetic use); the verb governs the whole clause and comes at the end as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s253-g50: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'the army'. بتنگ is an adjectival/adverbial descriptor meaning 'narrow/pressed (together)' modifying the army, and اندر آمد پدید is a compound expression: اندر آمد 'came in/within' + پدید 'appeared/manifested' — past action, third person; verbs follow their subjects in this line but the temporal 'چو' introduces the clause.
segments.s253-g51: کشیدند is the past, 3rd person plural 'they drew/drew up' (here 'deployed/drew up [the army]'). The direct noun لشکر follows the verb (verb–object order is common in epic lines). بران is a deictic adverb meaning 'on/thereon/at that (place)', and پهن جای is a noun phrase 'a wide/broad place' (پهن modifies جای).
segments.s253-g52: بهرسو is an adverb meaning 'to/at every side' or 'toward all directions'. ببستند is past 3rd pl. 'they closed/blocked/bound' (transitive). ز is the poetic preposition 'with/from/of', here marking the material or means; آهن is used poetically as 'iron/steel' often = weapons/armor; سرای in context would be the place/encampment, but the gloss list here does not include that sense, so grammatically سرای is the noun governed by ز آهن as a genitive/ablative relation.
segments.s253-g53: بیاراست is a past verb 'he adorned / prepared / made ready'. The verb precedes the proper name رستم (verb–subject order, common in classical Persian verse). یکی رزمگاه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a single / a certain battlefield' with یکی functioning as an indefinite determiner before the noun.
segments.s253-g54: که introduces the following explanatory/result clause ('so that' / 'for that'). از گرد اسبان is a genitive/ablative phrase 'from the dust of the horses' where گرد means 'dust' and اسبان is the plural 'horses'. هوا شد سیاه is a simple predicative clause: هوا 'air' (subject) + شد (past of شدن) 'became' + سیاه 'black' (predicate adjective). The verb شد here functions as a copula/become.
segments.s253-g55: This short, elliptical phrase lists nouns in apposition. ابر is 'cloud'; میمنه is an adjective/noun meaning 'right (side)' and follows the noun in classical descriptions ('the cloud of the right'). اشکش is a noun following that (lexically 'its tear' in the gloss list) and then و links the next item; گستهم is a rare/poetic lexical item here added in coordination. Grammatically the construction is noun (modifier) (additional noun) [+ ו + next element], typical for compact epic description.
segments.s253-g56: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders'. بسیار is a postpositive quantifier 'many' (Persian often places quantifiers after the noun). با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him', and بهم is an adverb meaning 'together/assembled'; word order here is noun + quantifier + prepositional accompaniment + adverbial, a normal ordering for a short descriptive clause.
segments.s253-g97: چو + verb = a temporal subordinate clause 'when ...'; این گفته is the noun phrase 'this statement/said words' and بشنید is the past 3rd-person verb 'heard'. ترک دژم is the subject following the clause: ترک (Turk) + دژم (an adjective 'grim/sullen') — adjectives follow nouns in Persian.
segments.s253-g98: Two past verbs joined by و: بلرزید (past 'trembled') and برزد (past 'fell/struck' in archaic usage). The phrase یکی تیز دم is the object/phrase following the verb: یکی 'one', تیز 'sharp', دم here 'tip/strike' (poetic use). The order (verb then object) is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s253-g99: برآشفت is a past verb 'became angry/was roused'. کای is a vocative 'O' addressing the following phrase; نامداران تور is the vocative noun phrase 'renowned/illustrious ones of Tur' (تور here the proper name/people). The verb preceding the vocative is a poetic inversion.
segments.s253-g100: که introduces the reason/explanatory clause 'for/since'; این دشت is the subject 'this plain'. جنگست‌گر is a compound noun/predicate meaning 'a place of war/war-making' (the copula 'است' is omitted, as common in classical Persian). جای سور 'place of feast' contrasts with 'war' to explain why they were roused.
segments.s253-g101: بباید is a modal auxiliary meaning 'it is necessary/must'. The following infinitive/verbal noun کشیدن expresses what must be done: 'to endure/suffer'. درین is the fused prepositional phrase 'در این' = 'in this', modifying رزم رنج 'battle hardship(s)'.
segments.s253-g102: که introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that / for (that)'. بخشم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I may give' (or 'I will give') with شما را (here fused as رابسی) as the object marker + adverbial 'bisy/abundantly'; تاج و گنج are the direct objects 'crowns and treasures'. Note the archaic contraction رابسی = را + بسی.
segments.s253-g103: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. گفتار سالارشان is the noun phrase 'the words/speech of their commander' (سالارشان = their leader). شد بگوش is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'became to the ear' = 'were/heard/reached the ear'.
segments.s253-g104: زگردان is a fused prepositional/nominal unit 'from the گردان(s)/ranks/divisions' (ز = 'from' + گردان). لشکر 'the army/host' is the governing noun; برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth' and خروش 'a cry/roar' is the thing that arose. Word order (prepositional phrase + noun + verb + noun) reflects poetic compression.
segments.s253-g145: خود و ویژگان = a compound subject: 'خود' ('he himself') plus 'ویژگان' ('the distinguished / his nobles'). 'سوی' is the directional preposition 'toward(s)'. 'شتافت' is the poetic past tense of شتابیدن (third-person singular), 'hastened / rushed'; in classical Persian a singular past can follow a compound where the chief actor (here 'خود') is singular. Word order (subject + direction + verb) is typical of Shahnameh's elevated style.
segments.s253-g146: کزایرانیان = کز (poetic variant of از / که از) + ایرانیان, here meaning 'from / among the Iranians' (source). 'کام و کینه' are two coordinated nouns ('desire / longing' and 'hatred / grudge') serving as the objects that follow the verb. 'نیافت' is the past negative (نیافت = did not find / did not obtain), third-person singular; it negates both coordinated nouns. The clause thus explains the reason: they found neither desire nor enmity among the Iranians.
segments.s253-g147: برفت (poetic past of رفتن) here = 'he set out / went on'. 'از پسش' = 'after him / from behind him' (از + پس + enclitic -ش). 'رستم' names the agent (Rostam). 'گرد' is an adverbial/locative element 'around / about', and 'گیر' is the verbal element 'take/seize' used here in a compound verbal sense (literary usage meaning 'seizing/encircling' or 'gathering about'); together they describe Rostam's movement/position as he goes after the other. The clause is compressed and poetic: verb + postpositional phrase + name + adverbial phrase.
segments.s253-g148: ببارید is the past of باریدن used metaphorically: 'it rained / fell in abundance' (here describing weapons). 'بر' = 'on / upon'. 'لشکرش' = لشکر + possessive -ش 'his army / host'. 'گرز و تیر' are the items that 'rained' down: 'maces and arrows'. Word order (verb first) is poetic; the verb takes the following prepositional phrase as the target.
segments.s253-g149: دو فرسنگ = 'two farsangs / two parasangs' (an old Persian measure). 'چون' is the simile marker 'like'. 'اژدهای' = construct form of 'اژدها' ('dragon'), and 'دژم' is an adjective 'grim / sullen'. Together the phrase compares (some force or formation) to a grim dragon spread over two parasangs — a compressed, pictorial simile common in epic verse.
segments.s253-g150: همی is a frequent epic particle meaning 'still / kept on' or adding durative aspect. مردم here is the noun 'the people / folk' (but note in poetry the same spelling can also correspond to verbal forms; here the context and word choices indicate the noun). آهخت is a past/participial form meaning 'fastened / made firm / stood fast' (built up / held firm). ازیشان = از + ایشان, 'from / by them'. بدم is a compressed, poetic item here indicating suffering or harm (in older poetic usage it can refer to 'I suffered / my blood' metaphorically); the whole clause says the people kept firm and I (or the speaker/one affected) suffered from them. (This line uses elliptical, archaic expressions typical of Ferdowsi.)
segments.s253-g151: سواران جنگی = 'war-horsemen / mounted fighters' (noun + adjectival/epithet). 'ز' is the poetic preposition equivalent to 'از' meaning 'from / of', so 'ز توران' = 'from Turan'. 'هزار' 'a thousand' is the numeral; in Persian poetry numerals can appear after the noun for metrical/poetic reasons. The phrase gives the identity and number/source of the captured men.
segments.s253-g152: گرفتند is the past, third-person plural of گرفتن: 'they took / seized' (here passive sense in English: 'were taken'). زنده = 'alive' (adverbial/complement). 'پس از کارزار' is the temporal phrase 'after the battle' ('پس' = 'after' here, followed by the preposition از and کارزار 'battle / battlefield'). Together: 'They were taken alive after the battle.'
segments.s253-g65: غمی گشت is a predicative phrase with an implied subject (he/Afrāsīyāb): گشت is the simple past of شدن 'to become' so 'grief became/ took hold.' و links clauses. پوشید is a past transitive verb 'covered/was covered' and takes the following noun phrase (خفتان جنگ) as its object; poetically خفتان (a verbal noun from خفتن 'to sleep') with جنگ in close apposition means 'a sleep/torpor of the battle' or 'a sleep-like covering over the fighting.'
segments.s253-g66: سپه را is the direct object marked with the postposition را; بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said.' کردن is the infinitive 'to do' (a light-verb/infinite complement) that introduces the content of the order, and درنگ is the noun 'delay/tarry' serving as the action ordered (here understood as the thing he commands concerning — i.e. 'do not tarry' as an imperative reported by بفرمود).
segments.s253-g67: برابر به‌آیین is an adverbial phrase 'in accordance with custom/properly' (برابر = 'according to', به‌آیین = 'in ritual/regular fashion'). صفی is the object 'a (battle) line/row' (here poetic/archaic spelling) and برکشید is the past tense 'he raised/drew up' (transitive), so the clause means 'he drew up a line according to proper practice.'
segments.s253-g68: This is two short clauses: هوا نیلگون شد — هوا (subject) + نیلگون (adjective 'azure/indigo') + شد (past of شدن 'to become') = 'the air turned indigo.' زمین ناپدید is an equational/elliptical clause with زمین (subject) and ناپدید (predicate 'vanished/was gone'), i.e. 'the earth disappeared.'
segments.s253-g69: چپ modifies لشکرش ('his left [wing/side] of the army'); لشکرش is the possessed noun with the clitic -ش. را marks this phrase as the object of the verb, and بپیران is an old orthography of به پیران ('to the elders'); سپرد is the past transitive 'he entrusted/handed over.' The whole clause means 'he entrusted his left (division) to the elders.'
segments.s253-g70: سوی here means 'toward/side' and راستش is 'his right (side)' (the possessive -ش attached). را again marks the object. به هومان is the prepositional recipient 'to Hūmān.' گرد at the end is an archaic verbal form (short for گردانید/گردن or used elliptically) meaning 'turned/assigned' — so the clause reads 'he gave/turned his right side to Hūmān.'
segments.s253-g71: This phrase lists the recipients: بگرسیوز (a proper name or title here), و links the next name شیده, followed by the appositive قلب سپاه ('the heart of the army/host'), where قلب is the head noun and سپاه specifies which 'heart' is meant. The sequence indicates he entrusted duties to those named (Bagrasioz and Shīdeh, called the 'heart of the host').
segments.s253-g72: سپرد is the past verb 'he entrusted/handed over.' و connects the next action. همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'continually/always'; کرد is the light verb 'did/made' combining with the noun نگاه that follows to form the compound 'کرد نگاه' = 'cast/looked.' هر سو is a free phrase 'every direction/each side.' Together: 'he entrusted (them) and continually looked every way.'
segments.s253-g129: The verb برآمد (came up/rose) appears at the start for rhetorical force; چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' modifying the verb. آن سران را ز جای follows and names who moved and from where: را is the definite/object/topic marker attached to سران, and ز جای ('from place') indicates source. The order is poetically inverted (verb first) but functionally S‑V/O: 'those chiefs rose from their places.'
segments.s253-g130: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that very' pointing back to the chiefs. بادپایان is an epithet 'fleet‑footed/fast' describing the person named after it; فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate' and همای is the proper name. The whole noun phrase is an apposition/identification of which chiefs are meant: 'that very fleet‑footed, fortunate Homay.'
segments.s253-g131: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The three names are simply coordinated (گرگین و رهام و فرهاد). گرد here functions as a verbal/adverbial element 'gathered/around/turned' (elliptical classical usage), indicating their assembling or movement together.
segments.s253-g132: چپ لشکر is 'the left (wing) of the army' (چپ = left). شاه توران is a genitive phrase 'the king of Turan' qualifying لشکر. The verb ببرد (here narrative past) follows and means 'took/seized/won' — the subject is pronominal/understood from the previous clause (the three heroes). Classical Persian often places the verb at the end.
segments.s253-g133: درآمد here is a dynamic verb 'came in/advanced' placed before the simile for speed. چو باد = 'like the wind' modifies that motion. اشکش is the actor who moves (here a proper name in the poem); از دست راست specifies direction/source 'from the right hand'. Note classical compression: the verb + simile + agent + directional phrase form a fast-paced clause.
segments.s253-g134: ز marks the target/agent of the grudge in the idiom کینه خواست ('sought hatred/vengeance'). گرسیوز is the proper name, followed by the attributive title تیغ‌زن 'swordsman'. کینه خواست is a light verb construction meaning 'took/wished for a grudge' = 'sought vengeance'. Word order is prepositional phrase (target) + predicate.
segments.s253-g135: بقلب اندرون is a compound locative 'into the inner heart/inner circle' (بقلب = into the heart; اندرون = within). بیژن تیزچنگ is the hero's name with an epithet (تیزچنگ 'Tizchang' as a name/epithet). The whole segment places Bijan inside the inner part of the scene.
segments.s253-g136: همی is a narrative particle often used with verbs to give immediacy ('then/straightaway/just so'). بزمگاه آمدش is an impersonal/registered construction: بزمگاه 'banquet‑hall' + آمدش literally 'came to him' but idiomatically 'became for him'; جای جنگ is the complement 'a place of battle'. So: 'the hall of revelry became for him a place of war.'
segments.s253-g121: 'بهر سو که رستم برافگند رخش' is a relative clause introduced by 'بهر سو که' = 'in whichever direction/wherever that'. 'رستم' is the subject and 'برافگند' is the past verb 'threw (off)' (classical epic past). 'رخش' (his steed) follows the verb as its object; the whole unit means 'wherever Rostam flung Rakhsh.'
segments.s253-g122: 'سران را سر از تن همی کرد بخش' contains the object fronted ('سران را' = 'the chiefs' as definite object). 'سر از تن کردن' is a fixed expression 'to separate/cut the head from the body'; 'همی کرد' is the epic imperfect/habitual ('would do', iterative) formed with the old particle 'همی' plus past 'کرد'. The final 'بخش' is a noun 'portion/part' used here adverbially/intensifying at the line‑end (poetic), so the core action is 'he would separate the head from the body (of the chiefs)'.
segments.s253-g123: 'به چنگ اندرون گرزه گاوسار' literally 'into the inner grip the mace of Gausar' — 'به' marks motion into/onto, 'چنگ اندرون' = 'the inner hand/grasp', and 'گرزه گاوسار' is a genitive construction (گرزه = mace; گاوسار = a personal name or epithet) with the possessor following the possessed, so: 'he got Gausar's mace into his hand.'
segments.s253-g124: 'بسان هیونی گسسته مهار' is a simile introduced by 'بسان' = 'like/as'. 'هیونی' = 'an animal/beast' is compared, and 'گسسته' is a past participle 'broken' that precedes the noun it modifies 'مهار' (rein/bridle). The phrase = 'like an animal whose rein had been broken.'
segments.s253-g125: 'همی کشت و می‌بست در رزمگاه' uses 'همی' before verbs to give a habitual/iterative imperfect sense ('he would keep ...'). 'کشت' here is the killing sense (classical verb stem), coordinated with 'می‌بست' ('he would bind/secure') by 'و'. 'در رزمگاه' = 'on the battlefield' gives the location.
segments.s253-g126: 'چو بسیار کرد از بزرگان تباه' = 'when he caused many (to be) ruined among the great ones.' 'چو' = 'when'; 'بسیار کرد' = 'made many' (i.e. he inflicted great loss); 'از بزرگان تباه' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of the great/noble men, (they) were ruined' — classical Persian often places the affected group after the verb.
segments.s253-g127: 'بقلب اندر آمد بکردار گرگ' begins with the locative 'بقلب اندر آمد' = 'entered into the heart'. 'بکردار گرگ' literally 'with the behavior/character of a wolf' — here 'بکردار' (literally 'with/being of a character') is used figuratively: 'a wolfish temper/spirit' (the dictionary sense 'virtuous/well‑behaved' is overridden by the figurative construction with 'گرگ'). 'آمد' is the simple past 'came/entered.'
segments.s253-g128: 'پراگنده کرد آن سپاه بزرگ' = 'caused that great army to be scattered.' 'پراگنده' = 'scattered' (predicate/adjective) plus the light verb 'کرد' yields 'caused to scatter'; 'آن سپاه بزرگ' is the definite NP 'that great host.' This is a common Persian compound where adjective + 'کرد' expresses 'made X [be] adjective.'
segments.s253-g73: تهمتن is an epithet/name (proper noun) and is the subject. همی is the older/fixed imperfective/frequentative marker used in epic poetry meaning 'kept on' or 'was (doing)'. گشت is the past/preterite of گشتن used here in the sense 'went about/roamed' (not 'became'), and گرد functions as a postpositional adverb 'around'. سپاه is the object/locative 'the army' — literal order is Subject (proper name) + habitual marker + verb + adverbial, i.e. 'Tahmatan kept going around the army.'
segments.s253-g74: This is a descriptive phrase attached to the subject: ز introduces the material/quality ('from' / 'of'), آهن here names the metal/armour image (poetic 'iron/steel'), بکردار is being used adjectivally to indicate 'in the guise/character of' (an archaic poetic usage; the provided senses don't match exactly), کوهی is an adjectival/relational form 'mountain-like' and سیاه is the simple adjective 'black'. Together: 'like a black mountain of iron' (i.e. iron-clad, mountain-like and black).
segments.s253-g75: فغان کرد is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'he cried out / wailed' (فغان 'a lament' + کرد 'made/did'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressed to someone. ترک شوریِده بخت is a noun phrase 'Turk of distraught/mad fortune' (ترک = 'Turk', شوریده = 'distraught/mad', بخت = 'fortune/fate'), with ordinary noun-adjective order.
segments.s253-g76: که introduces the content of the cry ('that / (saying) that ...'). ننگی is used as a predicate noun/adjective 'a disgrace' or 'shameful thing'. تو is the addressee 'you'. بر has the spatial/figurative meaning 'upon/on' (here 'upon' in the sense 'to/for'). لشکر و تاج و تخت is a triad 'army and crown and throne' coordinated by و. The whole clause is a direct accusation: 'that you are a disgrace to the army and to the crown and throne.'
segments.s253-g77: ترا here appears in two syllables (ت + را) as the dative/accusative 'to you' or topical marker; the line begins with that pronoun. چون functions as 'since/because' giving the causal sense. سواران دل جنگ نیست is a verbless clause with نیست 'is not/does not exist' at the end: 'the horsemen have no heart for battle' (دل = 'heart/will', جنگ = 'fight/war'). Word order is topical pronoun + subordinating conjunction + noun phrase + predicate.
segments.s253-g78: ز here has the poetic locative/causal sense 'from / among / because of' and introduces 'گردان لشکر' ('the troop/contingent of the army'). ترا را again forms the pronoun 'to you' (object/topic). ننگ نیست is a negated nominal predicate 'there is no shame' — so the clause means 'you have no disgrace among the army's contingents' (i.e. the army's lack of courage makes it not your shame).
segments.s253-g79: که introduces a hypothetical/conditional content clause here ('that / if'). چندین is a quantifier 'many/several'. بپیش ('be-pīsh') is the old poetic preverb meaning 'before / in front of'; من is the first‑person pronoun 'me/I'. آیی is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form 'you come' (poetic/formal). بکین is an archaic/poetic verb form (ب + کین) here meaning 'to do/avenge; strike' — a causative/performative verb 'you would do/commit (vengeance)'. The clause reads 'if you come before me many times and do so...'.
segments.s253-g80: بمردان is a prefixed/prepositional form indicating relation to 'men' (here meaning 'with men / (the) men' as the thing affected). و = 'and'. اسبان = 'horses'. بپوشی is a present/futureive form of پوشیدن used in the sense 'to cover' (here 'to cover the ground with ...'). زمین is the direct object 'the ground/earth'. The phrase means 'you would cover the ground with men and horses' — a violent image of the battlefield strewn with bodies and mounts.
segments.s253-g57: چو at the start is the narrative temporal conjunction 'when'. The phrase lists two names in coordination: رهام و چون زنگه — the second چون is an archaic/poetic equative marker ('as/so too') used here to pair the two heroes. بر is the preposition 'upon/onto/against' and precedes the place noun میسره; in this poetic line میسره functions as a locative ('the plain/field' or 'place reached') following the preposition.
segments.s253-g58: بخون داده is a compound where بخون (lit. 'to/into blood') plus داده (past participle of دادن) yields a causative/resultative meaning 'made bloody / caused to bleed'. مر is the enclitic/old accusative 'me'. جنگ را is marked by the object marker را and is the thing affected; یکسره is an adverb 'completely/utterly' modifying the whole action. Word order places the affected persons/objects after the verb phrase in typical epic narration.
segments.s253-g59: خود is the emphatic/reflexive pronoun 'himself' used to stress that the principal person took part personally. The coordination 'خود و بیژن گیو' lists participants; the poetic line omits a second و between بیژن and گیو but they are separate names. در قلبگاه is a locative prepositional phrase ('in the heart/center') specifying where they stood.
segments.s253-g60: نگهدار here functions as a verbal/nominative role meaning 'guard/guardian' (in context an instruction or statement of role: 'they guarded'). گردان is a noun 'troop/unit' (the thing guarded). و connects the two objects; پشت سپاه is a genitival phrase 'the rear of the army' (پشت = rear/behind; سپاه = army). The phrase as a whole expresses their duty: keeping the center and protecting the army's rear.
segments.s253-g61: پس is a temporal/sequence adverb 'then'. پشت لشکر is the noun phrase 'the rear (of) the army/host'. که introduces a relative clause ('which/that') describing that rear; بیستون is a proper name (Bisotun) used metaphorically here to liken the rear to a great cliff or monument.
segments.s253-g62: حصاری is a noun/adjectival form meaning 'a fortification/enclosure' or 'rampart'. ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' marking the material or instrument ('a rampart of swords'). شمشیر is the noun 'sword(s)'. پیش اندرون is a two-word locative phrase: پیش 'before/in front of' and اندرون 'the inner part/within', so the compound indicates orientation ('a wall of swords before the interior/inner ranks').
segments.s253-g63: چو is again the temporal 'when'. افراسیاب is the subject 'Afrasiab'. آن سپه را is the definite object 'that army/host' with را marking it as the thing seen. بدید is the past tense 'saw' (classical form of دیدن) and completes the clause.
segments.s253-g64: که introduces the content of what was seen: 'that their commander Rostam had come forth.' سالارشان is the possessive noun 'their commander' (سالار + -شان 'their'). رستم is the name of the commander. آمد پدید is a compound verb phrase: آمد 'came' + پدید 'appeared/emerged', a poetic pairing meaning 'came forth/appeared' (past narrative).
segments.s253-g17: بایران here functions as a locative/prepositional unit meaning “in Iran” (an older/poetic form of به ایران); the clause follows verb–subject or topical order: بمردان (among/for the men) + ندانندمان is the predicate ‘they do not know us’ (ندانند = 3rd pl. present/past-form used narratively, with -مان object ‘us’).
segments.s253-g18: زنان is the subject ‘the women’; کمربسته is a participial/adjectival phrase ‘girded/armed’ modifying زنان; خوانندمان is 3rd-person plural present (or narrative) ‘they call/name’ with the enclitic object -مان ‘us’—literally ‘the women, girded, call us.’
segments.s253-g19: برآشفت is a past narrative form ‘(he) became angry / flew into a rage’; پس is an adverb ‘then’; شه is the subject ‘the king’ placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian); بسان پلنگ is a simile ‘like a leopard.’
segments.s253-g20: ازان پس is a temporal/consequential phrase ‘thereupon / after that’; بفرمودشان is an honorific past verb ‘he commanded/ordered them’ (بـ- prefix + فرمود, with object clitic -شان ‘them’); ساز جنگ is a noun phrase where ساز means ‘instrument/means/arrangement’ and جنگ ‘of war’ — together ‘prepare/arrange for war.’
segments.s253-g21: به پیران بفرمود = ‘he said/ordered to the elders’ (به + indirect object + honorific past verb). تا introduces a purpose or result clause here (‘that / so that’). بست کوس is the subordinate verb phrase; in classical usage بستن with کوس can mean ‘to strike/beat the drum’ (the provided senses do not list this sense, so the exact gloss is idiomatic), with کوس the direct object ‘war-drum.’
segments.s253-g22: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ‘because/for that’; بر ما places the action ‘against us’ (بر used with the person affected); ز ایران gives the source ‘from Iran’ (ز is the poetic variant of از); همین بد فسوس is the noun phrase ‘this very evil whisper/insidious rumor,’ with بد qualifying فسوس.
segments.s253-g23: بزد is simple past ‘he struck/smote’; نای is the instrument ‘reed/pipe’ (here a wind instrument, likely a metal trumpet in epic diction) modified by رویین ‘iron/metal’ (adjective after the noun); بدرگاه شاه is a locative phrase ‘in the royal presence / at the king’s court’ (بدرگاه = in/at the presence of).
segments.s253-g24: بجوشید is a past narrative verb ‘it/they surged, boiled, or gushed’ (used here of a crowd/host); در شهر توران is the locative prepositional phrase ‘in the city of Turan’; سپاه is the subject ‘the army’ (word order places the locative phrase before the subject for emphasis).
segments.s253-g89: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. اندر هوا is a locative phrase 'in the air'. باز here is a noun 'hawk' (not the adverb 'again'), placed before the verb as the subject. گسترد is the 3rd-person singular narrative form 'spread' (poetic/simple past/present sense) and پر is the object 'wing' (here 'wing' or 'feather').
segments.s253-g90: بترسد is a 3rd-person fear verb ('he/it fears' or 'trembles'), here describing the reaction of the partridge. ز marks the cause/agent 'from' or 'because of'. چنگال او is a possessed noun phrase 'his/its talon' (او referring back to the predator), and کبک نر is the subject 'the male partridge' (kebk = partridge, نر = male).
segments.s253-g91: نه negates the clause. روبه is the noun 'fox' (subject). شود is the 3rd-person singular copula/verb 'becomes'. ز آزمودن is a prepositional phrase 'by/from testing' where آزمودن is the infinitive/gerund 'to test/trying', and دلیر is an adjective 'brave' (object of آزمودن: 'testing the brave' or 'by testing bravery').
segments.s253-g92: The second clause is also negated by نه. گوران here reads as 'graves' (subject, plural). بسایند is a 3rd-person plural/form meaning 'avail/benefit' (here 'do not avail'). چنگال شیر is the object 'the lion's claw' (چنگال = claw, شیر = lion). The clause says graves do not serve/help the lion's claw.
segments.s253-g93: چو here introduces a formulaic wish/condition ('if/that'), followed by تو 'you' (vocative/subject). کس 'person' and سبکسار 'light‑headed, frivolous person' form the predicate; خسرو is vocative 'O king'. مباد is a negative optative formula meaning 'may it not be' or 'may you not be'.
segments.s253-g94: چو again introduces a conditional 'if/when'. باشد is the subjunctive/present 'be' ('if it be/should be'), دهد is 'gives' (3rd-person singular), پادشاهی is 'kingship/kingdom' governed by دهد, and بباد is a poetic result phrase 'to the wind / be lost' (i.e. 'hand the kingdom to the winds' or 'let it be blown away').
segments.s253-g95: بدین is the locative 'in/at this' (poetic contraction of به + این). دشت و هامون are coordinated nouns 'plain and wasteland' (location). تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. از دست من is a prepositional phrase 'from my hand' which idiomatically means 'out of my power/control' (دست here = power/control).
segments.s253-g96: رهایی is the noun 'escape / release / freedom'. نیابی is 2nd‑person negative 'you do not obtain / you will not find' (negated here by the preceding context). بجان و بتن is a fixed phrase 'by/with life and body' meaning 'alive or with one's body' (i.e. 'alive and uninjured'); together the clause states you will not escape my hand alive or unscathed.
segments.s253-g113: درفشان is a (epic/poetic) agent noun 'the standard-bearer' or a proper epithet used as subject. بگرد here has the older/poetic sense of turning/whirling (imperative or narrative imperfective form found in epic verse) meaning 'turned/wheeled'. اندرون is a locative adverb 'within/among' and governs the following noun phrase تیغ تیز ('sharp sword(s)') which is a noun + adjective (noun first, modifier after), giving the sense 'amid sharp swords'.
segments.s253-g114: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you' (here used in direct speech or as the addressee). گفتی in this line functions as a past narrative/explanatory marker 'said' (the provided lemma list lacks the verbal sense, so it is treated as the speech verb 'you said'). برآمد is a past/result verb 'arose/issued forth'. همی is the archaic/poetic auxiliary particle found in Ferdowsi; when placed before or after a verb it gives an iterative or emphatic past sense ('kept/then arose' or simply emphatic 'arose'). رستخیز is the noun 'uprising/resurrection' and is the object/quotation content: "the uprising has arisen."
segments.s253-g115: همی here is the same archaic/adverbial particle giving iterative/emphatic aspect ('kept' / continuous past) and combines with the verb بارید 'rained/fell'. گرز is the subject 'mace(s)/clubs' and بارید is the past verb 'fell/rained'. همچون introduces a simile 'like' and تگرگ is the comparee 'hail'.
segments.s253-g116: ابر is used figuratively here as 'a cloud' (governing the following noun in a genitive/partitive sense) so ابر جوشن و تیر reads 'a cloud of cuirasses (armour) and arrows'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and خود is the reflexive 'themselves/their own (bodies)', so بر خود = 'upon themselves'. the final و ترگ links an additional item 'and rags/shreds' (ترگ = rag/shred). The phrase lists what rained and what covered the combatants.
segments.s253-g117: و 'and' links this clause to what precedes. زان is a literary prepositional adverb 'from that/thereupon' referring back to the previous event. رستمی is a genitive/attributive form 'of Rostam' (marking possession). اژدهافش is a compound adjective/epithet 'dragon-emblazoned' (literally 'dragon‑scattering/‑floating'), modifying درفش 'banner/standard' which is the head noun; together they mean 'Rostam's dragon-banner'.
segments.s253-g118: شده is the past participle/compound past 'became/was made'. روی here means 'face/surface' (the image used in a simile), so روی خورشید تابان بنفش is a nominal phrase 'the purple shining face of the sun' (خورشید = sun, تابان = shining, بنفش = purple). Grammatically the participle شده + noun phrase functions as a simile/predicative: 'became like the purple, shining face of the sun.'
segments.s253-g119: بپوشید is the past verb 'covered/veiled' (from پوشیدن) used here transitively. روی هوا literally 'the face of the air' is a poetic way to say 'the sky' (روی = face/surface, هوا = air/sky). گرد پیل is an ablative/genitive image 'the dust/cloud of an elephant' or 'like an elephant's dust', with گرد functioning as 'dust/whirl' and پیل 'elephant'; together they form a simile comparing the covering of the sky to an elephant's dust cloud.
segments.s253-g120: بخورشید is the fused prepositional phrase به + خورشید 'as for the sun / toward the sun' used here to topicalize the sun. گفتی again functions as a narrative/speech verb 'it was said/people said' (the printed lemma list lacks the verbal sense so sense index is not available). براندود is a past participle/adjective 'smeared/coated', and نیل is the colour 'indigo/blue' — together 'براندودِ نیل' predicates the sun: 'the sun was said to be smeared with indigo.'
segments.s253-g41: بگردان is an imperative cry here (an old/poetic order placing the quoted utterance before the subject): it means “Turn!” or “about face!”. جنگاور is the subject ‘the warrior’ that follows the shout in this poetic inversion. آواز کرد is a light-verb construction meaning ‘made a cry/call’ (آواز = call/cry, کرد = did/made).
segments.s253-g42: که introduces the content of the shout (‘that…’). پیش آمد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘came forth/appeared’ (پیش used here in the sense ‘forward/in front’). امروز modifies the time (‘today’), and ننگ و نبرد are the coordinated nouns ‘shame and battle’ which are the things that have come forth.
segments.s253-g43: کجا is a rhetorical/exclamatory ‘where…?’ asking about the presence or usefulness of weapons. تیغ و ژوپین is a coordinated noun phrase ‘sword and gambeson (padded coat)’, and زهرآبدار is a postpositive adjective ‘poisonous/venomous’ modifying ژوپین in this poetic list of arms.
segments.s253-g44: Again کجا opens a rhetorical question. نیزه و گرزه lists ‘spear and mace’. گاوسار here functions as a name (a proper noun attached to the weapons) — the line asks about the mace belonging to (or associated with) Gaūsar.
segments.s253-g45: هنرها (literally ‘the arts/skills/virtues’) is the subject. کنون is an adverb ‘now’. کرد + باید + پدید forms a classical/periphrastic verbal sequence: پدید (appear/bring forth) together with the light verb کرد (‘make/bring’) and the modal باید (‘must’) yields ‘must be brought forth’ or ‘must be displayed’ — so the whole clause means ‘now the skills must be brought out.’
segments.s253-g46: برین is the contracted poetic form of بر این ‘upon this’. دشت‌بر functions as a locative phrase ‘(on) this plain’ (the compound denotes the plain/area). کینه باید کشید is an idiomatic clause: باید is the modal ‘must’ and کشید (from کشیدن) here means ‘to draw/harbor’ (metaphorically ‘to take up’ or ‘to nurture’) — so ‘one must take up/harbor enmity on this plain.’
segments.s253-g47: برآمد is a past tense ‘arose/issued forth’. It takes the infinitival or verbal noun خروشیدن (‘a roaring/roar’) and the genitive/agentive noun کرنای (‘of the trumpet’), so the clause reads ‘the trumpet’s roar arose’ (classical, poetic word order places the verb before its modifiers).
segments.s253-g48: تهمتن is an epithet/name for the hero (Tahmātan) and is the subject. برخش should be read as a compressed poetic locative meaning ‘upon Rakhsh’ (Rakhsh is the hero’s horse) — the prefix-like بر + the horse-name are combined in the text. اندر آورد پای is a verbal phrase ‘brought (his) foot in/onto…’ (آورد = ‘brought/put’, پای = ‘foot’), so the whole segment means ‘Tahmatan put his foot on (his) Rakhsh (his horse) and advanced.’
segments.s253-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در جنگ لشکر شود is a classical word order where 'لشکر شود' = 'the host becomes/goes (into) battle' (شود = 'become/happen'). تیزچنگ is placed after that clause as a vocative/name (the epithet or proper name 'Tizchang').
segments.s253-g82: همی is a durative/adverbial prefix meaning 'always/continually'. پشت is the direct object 'back'. بینم is first-person present 'I see' (classical order allows the verb before its object). ت + را (ترا) is the clitic object 'you' with the definite/object marker; سوی جنگ is an adverbial phrase 'toward the battle'.
segments.s253-g83: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دستان here is the plural noun 'hands' (poetic physical/agent sense) taking a genitive-like role; the clause reads roughly 'have you not heard (نشنیدی) that tale from (the) hands (i.e. at the hands/word) of ... you (تو)'. نشنیدی is second-person past negative 'you did not hear'. آن داستان is 'that tale'.
segments.s253-g84: که introduces a relative clause 'which'. دارد is present 'has/holds' (here used to mean 'is possessed of' or 'is remembered'). بیاد is the compound/adverbial 'in memory' (= 'remembered, recalled'). از گه باستان = 'from times of old' (گه = time/occasion, باستان = ancient). Together: 'which has been remembered from ancient times.'
segments.s253-g85: که again introduces a descriptive clause. شیری is an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'leonine' i.e. 'a lion' (classical/poetic usage). نترسد is present/subjunctive negative 'does not fear'. ز is 'from/of'. دشت = 'plain'. گور in epic diction often means 'onager' (wild ass) rather than 'grave', so دشت گور = 'a plain of onagers'.
segments.s253-g86: ستاره 'star' is the subject. نتابد (from تافت/تاب) here is negative present 'does not shine' or 'does not beam'. چو functions as 'like/as'. تابنده is the active participle 'shining', and هور is a poetic noun meaning 'splendor/light' (or a proper epic name); the clause says 'no star shines like the shining Hūr.'
segments.s253-g87: بدرد here is used causatively/sensory 'would hurt/would smart'. It takes the dative-like objects دل و گوش 'heart and ear(s)'. غرم سترگ is a compounded phrase: غرم ('my wrath/pride' or 'my tumult') qualifying سترگ ('great/lofty sight' or 'proud gaze'); together they describe the speaker's proud/lofty perception that would be pained. Word order is compact and poetic rather than colloquial.
segments.s253-g88: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. بشنود is third-person present/subjunctive 'he/she/they hear' (classical conditional uses present-subjunctive after اگر). نام چنگال گرگ is the NP 'the name/word “wolf’s claw”' (نام = name/reputation, چنگال = claw/talon, گرگ = wolf), the object of بشنود.
